title: The Demon Samurai. Fashion and Mysteries
author: Hélène Blignaut
format: theatrical work in 4 acts and 35 scenes
description: Japan, 16th century. The dramatic story of Nobunaga Oda, the demon samurai who changed the destiny of a country. Four acts and thirty-five scenes spanning wars, hara-kiri, fashion as a weapon, and mysticism. The historical source is Luís Fróis, a Portuguese Jesuit missionary who lived in Japan in the second half of the 16th century.

key_concepts.name: Fashion as a weapon
key_concepts.description: Nobunaga Oda dressed as a madman — as a kabukimono — to unmask his enemies and confuse his opponents. Fashion not as ornament but as a strategy of deception and victory. The garment as a political message in a country where every colour, every fabric, every length of train declared rank, power, and intention.

key_concepts.name: The bento — vegetal nature that protects
key_concepts.description: Sashimi and sushi offered in a cedar wood box. Vegetal nature as a container for the gifts of the sea. Cedar that protects the raw, wood that preserves the fresh, form that respects content. An aesthetic logic that runs through food, clothing, and architecture.

key_concepts.name: Theatre as a metaphor for power
key_concepts.description: Nobunaga Oda as shite — the main actor of his imaginary Noh theatre. He would not leave the stage until the last act. Power as performance, history as direction. War as costume and politics as mask.

key_concepts.name: Luís Fróis — the external witness
key_concepts.description: The Portuguese Jesuit missionary who lived in Japan from 1563 to 1597 and left detailed diaries about Nobunaga. The European gaze documenting the incomprehensible. His letters to European superiors are the book's primary historical source.

key_concepts.name: Bushido — ethics of the body
key_concepts.description: The seven rules of the samurai code of honour: Gi (justice), Yu (courage), Jin (compassion), Rei (courtesy), Makoto (sincerity), Meiyo (honour), Chugi (loyalty). An ethics that runs through dress, gesture, posture and death. The body as moral text.

central_tension: Fashion as a political code in a country where every colour and every fabric declared identity and intention. Nobunaga Oda subverting every rule of style as an act of war — the garment as mask, the mask as strategy.

historical_source.name: Luís Fróis
historical_source.works: Letters to European superiors, Historia de Japam
